elements.sent-2411-001: Cross-view geo-localization (CVGL), which involves matching and retrieving satellite images to determine the geographic location of a ground image, is crucial in GNSS-constrained scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, this task faces significant challenges due to substantial viewpoint discrepancies, the complexity of localization scenarios, and the need for global localization.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address these issues, we propose a novel CVGL framework that integrates the vision foundational model DINOv2 with an advanced feature mixer.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our framework introduces the symmetric InfoNCE loss and incorporates near-neighbor sampling and dynamic similarity sampling strategies, significantly enhancing localization accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-005: Experimental results show that our framework surpasses existing methods across multiple public and self-built datasets.
elements.sent-2411-006: To further improve global-scale performance, we have developed CV-Cities, a novel dataset for global CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-007: CV-Cities includes $223,736$ ground-satellite image pairs with geolocation data, spanning sixteen cities across six continents and covering a wide range of complex scenarios, providing a challenging benchmark for CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-008: The framework trained with CV-Cities demonstrates high localization accuracy in various test cities, highlighting its strong globalization and generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our datasets and codes are available at \url{https://github.com/GaoShuang98/CVCities .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Cross-view geo-localization (CVGL) is a new positioning technique that does not rely on GNSS signals but speculates the location by comparing the similarity between ground and pre-acquired satellite images.
elements.sent-2411-011: Since satellite images can be conveniently acquired globally, this method is widely applicable globally and shows significant advantages in urban canyon user localization, automated driving, event detection, military intelligence acquisition, drone navigation, disaster response, etc. Nevertheless, despite the recent advancements in CVGL techniques, research in this field still presents some challenges.
elements.sent-2411-012: The effective extraction and matching of image features with different viewpoints represent a complex problem, particularly in environments characterized by dense buildings, seasonal changes, or rapidly developing urban landscapes\cite{ref5 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Furthermore, enhancing the robustness and accuracy of CVGL to accommodate diverse and intricate global scenes represents a pivotal avenue of current research.
elements.sent-2411-014: The rapid development of vision foundational models has made generating generalized visual features from images possible.
elements.sent-2411-015: Due to the training using hundreds of millions of data, the vision foundational model can extract image features that are more general and robust than those extracted by traditional models.
elements.sent-2411-016: This enables it to cope effectively with the huge viewpoint differences in CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-017: This paper proposes a new framework for CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-018: It employs the vision foundational model DINOv2\cite{ref6 with a feature mix module\cite{ref7 as a model to extract robust global features.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The effective symmetric InfoNCE loss\cite{ref8, ref9, ref10 is used for training, and near-neighbor sampling and dynamic similarity sampling strategies are employed to improve the model’s accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Most existing CVGL datasets are limited to a specific region, with a relatively narrow range of scenes and limited data.
elements.sent-2411-021: This constrains the model’s global generalization performance.
elements.sent-2411-022: This paper presents CV-Cities (Cross-View geo-localization in global Cities), a global CVGL dataset, to improve the model’s generalization performance.
elements.sent-2411-023: The results of the experiments show that the CV-Cities markedly enhance the model’s generalization ability.
elements.sent-2411-024: Following testing in a variety of cities across the globe, the model showed high geo-localization accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-025: Furthermore, the test set included in CV-Cities encompasses a multitude of intricate scenarios, including city, natural, seasonal change, water area, occlusion, and more.
elements.sent-2411-026: This represents a novel and formidable benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our key contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-028: We have established a global CVGL dataset, CV-Cities, which offers the most comprehensive coverage and diverse range of images available for CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-029: The dataset facilitates enhanced geo-localization accuracy and generalization performance for the model.
elements.sent-2411-030: CV-Cities encompass a range of complex scenarios, and the test and training set do not intersect, thereby providing a highly challenging benchmark for CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-031: We propose a novel CVGL framework employing a model with shared weight parameters to extract features from two viewpoints images.
elements.sent-2411-032: This framework does not require the processing of images, such as polar coordinate conversion or image generation, which results in a simple structure and higher accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-033: Furthermore, the framework’s performance has been evaluated, resulting in the highest CVGL accuracy to date.
elements.sent-2411-034: Two sampling strategies, near neighbor sampling (NNS) and dynamic similarity sampling (DSS), were employed to mine negative samples for model training.
elements.sent-2411-035: This approach improves the accuracy of the framework’s localization.
elements.sent-2411-036: A comparison was conducted between the CV-Cities and other datasets, demonstrating that the CV-Cities offers a significant advantage in improving model generalization.
elements.sent-2411-037: In the early stages of CVGL, manual features were employed to facilitate the matching process between ground and satellite images.
elements.sent-2411-038: For instance, Lin et al.\cite{ref11 initially employed manual features, including HOG\cite{ref12 , Gist\cite{ref13 , and color histogram, for CVGL.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Viswanathan\cite{ref14 transformed ground query images into satellite images and matched them using manual features, such as SIFT\cite{ref15 , SURF\cite{ref16 , and FREAK\cite{ref17 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Although manual features are simple and interpretable, they struggle with viewpoint discrepancies, resulting in low matching accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-041: With the rise of deep learning, CNN-based methods became dominant.
elements.sent-2411-042: Lin et al.\cite{ref18 introduced Where-CNN, achieving $80\%$ top $20\%$ accuracy, but highlighting the need for improvement in practical applications.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Workman et al.\cite{ref19 enhanced geo-localization by optimizing the CNN architecture and introducing the CVUSA dataset, now a key benchmark in CVGL.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Tian et al.\cite{ref20 applied Faster R-CNN\cite{ref21 for object detection, followed by twin neural networks for retrieval and a dominance set-based matching method.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Hu et al.\cite{ref22 developed CVM-Net, which uses convolutional layers with NetVLAD\cite{ref23 encoding to extract global features.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: More recently, Deuser et al.\cite{ref8 proposed Sample4Geo, utilizing ConvNeXt with symmetric InfoNCE loss, demonstrating high performance across multiple datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Li et al.\cite{ref23-1 used CNN as the backbone, and proposed a framework called patch similarity self-knowledge distillation.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Despite these advancements, challenges in accuracy and efficiency persist, as CNN-based methods like Faster R-CNN are computationally expensive, and models like CVM-Net and Sample4Geo still face generalization issues across datasets.
elements.sent-2411-049: The introduction of Vision Transformers (ViT) has significantly improved cross-view matching accuracy by leveraging self-attention mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-050: Dai et al.\cite{ref24 introduced a feature segmentation and region alignment structure to enhance contextual understanding.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Yang et al.\cite{ref25 utilized ViT’s self-attention to model global dependencies, reducing visual variability.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Other works, like Zhuang et al.\cite{ref26 , integrated pixel-based attention for UAV-to-remote sensing image matching, and Wang et al.\cite{ref27 combined CNN and transformer networks to improve feature embedding.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Zhang et al.\cite{ref28 proposed the GeoDTR model to separate geometric information from visual features using a transformer-based extractor.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Despite their accuracy, ViT-based models are computationally intensive and prone to overfitting on small datasets, limiting scalability.
elements.sent-2411-055: Geometric transformations and image generation methods have also been explored to minimize viewpoint differences.
elements.sent-2411-056: For example, Shi et al.\cite{ref2 used polar coordinate transformations to convert satellite images into panoramic views, while Zhao et al.\cite{ref29 employed geometric projections to transform ground panoramic images into satellite viewpoints.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Regmi et al.\cite{ref30 and Hao et al.\cite{ref31 utilized GANs for cross-view image conversion, with some methods achieving high fidelity but also introducing artifacts that can degrade accuracy.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Toker et al.\cite{ref32 proposed a GAN-based retrieval method, and Huang et al.\cite{ref33 introduced a sequential fork network for generating ground images from satellite views.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: However, these methods face limitations, as GAN-generated images often suffer from artifacts, and geometric transformations only partially reduce viewpoint discrepancies.
elements.sent-2411-060: Moreover, the GAN generation step can also reduce the overall efficiency of the CVGL process.
elements.sent-2411-061: The CVGL dataset utilized for training and testing is summarized in Table \ref{tab1 .}
elements.sent-2411-062: CVUSA\cite{ref19 comprises ground and satellite images sourced from various regions within the United States.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The ground images are derived from Google Street View, with a resolution of $1,232 \times 224$ pixels, while the satellite images are obtained from Microsoft Bing Maps, with a zoom level of $19$ and a resolution of $750 \times 750$ pixels.
elements.sent-2411-064: The dataset encompasses $35,532$ and $8,884$ pairs of images for training and testing, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-065: It represents the inaugural dataset to be used in a cross-view matching study.
elements.sent-2411-066: The ground images included in this dataset have been aligned with the orientation of the satellite images and thus can be used as an additional feature.
elements.sent-2411-067: CVACT\cite{ref34 provides a collection of Canberra ground and satellite images.}
elements.sent-2411-068: The ground panoramic images are captured from Google Street View, with a resolution of $1,664 \times 832$ pixels.
elements.sent-2411-069: The satellite images are derived from Google Maps, with a $1,200 \times 1,200$ pixels resolution.
elements.sent-2411-070: The dataset comprises $35,532$ and $8,884$ pairs of images for training and evaluation (CVACT Val).
elements.sent-2411-071: Moreover, CVACT furnishes $92,802$ pairs of test images (CVACT Test) to buttress fine-grained urban-scale CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-072: University-1652\cite{ref35 comprises three types of images: drone, satellite, and ground views of $1,652$ buildings from $72$ universities across the globe.}
elements.sent-2411-073: On average, each building is represented by $54$ drone images, $3.38$ ground images, and one satellite image.
elements.sent-2411-074: This is the inaugural UAV-based geo-localization dataset that supports target localization and UAV navigation tasks from the UAV viewpoint.
elements.sent-2411-075: VIGOR\cite{ref36 comprises $105,214$ ground panoramic images and $90,618$ satellite images encompassing four major United States cities (Seattle, New York, San Francisco, and Chicago).}
elements.sent-2411-076: A query image of this dataset may correspond to multiple reference images, and the query image is not necessarily located at the query image center.
elements.sent-2411-077: The dataset has two particular settings: the SAME, in which images from all cities can be used in training and testing, and the CROSS, in which training is performed in Seattle and New York and evaluation is performed in Chicago and San Francisco.
elements.sent-2411-078: CV-Cities (ours) comprises $223,736$ ground panoramic images and an equal number of satellite images all accompanied by high-precision GPS coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-079: These images represent sixteen representative cities across five continents.
elements.sent-2411-080: The ground images are $360^{\circ}$ panorama images with a resolution of $4,096 \times 2,048$ pixels, while the resolution of satellite images is $746 \times 746$ pixels, and are captured at a zoom level of $20$.
elements.sent-2411-081: The spatial resolution is $0.298 m$, corresponding to a latitude and longitude range of $0.002 \times 0.002^{\circ}$ (about $222 \times 222 m$).
elements.sent-2411-082: The images of each city in the dataset can be used for training and testing purposes.

elements.sent-2411-083: The most advanced CVGL methods\cite{ref8, ref37,ref38,ref39,ref40 are primarily trained and tested using CVUSA, CVACT, VIGRO, and University-1652 datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-084: However, these datasets are not without limitations.
elements.sent-2411-085: For instance, although the images in the CVUSA are widely distributed in the United States, the distribution density is low, and the distance between images is considerable.
elements.sent-2411-086: While the CVACT dataset comprises more images, they are only densely distributed in Melbourne, Australia.
elements.sent-2411-087: Consequently, it is challenging for the trained models to adapt to scenarios with disparate styles globally.
elements.sent-2411-088: The distribution of images in the VIGRO is constrained to four cities within the United States, such as Chicago and New York City, which is confined within the United States.
elements.sent-2411-089: University-1652 has a limited number of images, and the images have a low resolution, lack GPS coordinates, and primarily feature buildings, which collectively prevent the model from learning universal cross-view features.
elements.sent-2411-090: Furthermore, the test set images of CVUSA and CVACT are distributed in the same region as the training set images.
elements.sent-2411-091: This results in images in the same area having similar scene styles, such as buildings, vegetation, and roads.
elements.sent-2411-092: These similarities prevent the model from accurately demonstrating its ability in generalized CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-093: Consequently, the test accuracy is high.
elements.sent-2411-094: For instance, the top-1 accuracy of our model in CVUSA has reached $99.19\%$, rendering this dataset difficult and unchallenging as a practical test benchmark for new methods.
elements.sent-2411-095: To address the issues above, we have constructed the CV-Cities dataset, which serves as a novel training and testing benchmark for the study of CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-096: Our CV-Cities dataset was constructed by combining ground and satellite images from Google Street View and Google Earth.
elements.sent-2411-097: To improve the geographic diversity and globalization of the data set, we selected sixteen representative cities from six continents worldwide to establish the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-098: These 16 cities cover a wide range of geographic environments from developed to developing cities, from coastal to inland, and from the southern hemisphere to the northern hemisphere.
elements.sent-2411-099: They encompass a wide spectrum of climates, including Mediterranean, highland mountainous, temperate, and tropical, each with unique vegetation and architectural characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-100: The distribution of these cities is illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig1 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-101: Ground images were collected in each city at a spacing of approximately $100$ meters.
elements.sent-2411-102: The image size was $4,096 \times 2,048$ pixels, and the field of view angle was $360^{\circ}$.
elements.sent-2411-103: The north direction of these ground images was randomized.
elements.sent-2411-104: The ground images were captured by six rolling-shutter cameras, which were either mounted on cars or carried by pedestrians.
elements.sent-2411-105: Various sensors were incorporated into the cameras to correct the image locations, ensuring that the locations of the ground images were accurate\cite{ref41 .}
elements.sent-2411-106: The satellite images are at level $20$, are $746 \times 746$ pixels in size, and have a spatial resolution of $0.298 m$.
elements.sent-2411-107: This corresponds to a latitude and longitude range of $0.002 \times 0.002^{\circ}$ (about $222 \times 222$ m).
elements.sent-2411-108: The sampling points are spaced approximately $100$ m apart, ensuring a certain degree of overlap between satellite images from neighboring sample points.
elements.sent-2411-109: The CV-Cities dataset has eight cities (sixteen in total), with the distribution of sample points in Fig. \ref{fig1 (b).}

elements.sent-2411-111: The sampling points encompass diverse scenes, including city streets, natural scenes, water scenes, occlusion, etc. These sample images are shown in Fig. \ref{fig2 .}
elements.sent-2411-112: To obtain the scenes distribution of CV-Cities, we selected 4,000 ground images from each scene within the CV-Cities to train a ViTb16 model, aiming to accurately predict the scene categories for all sampling points in CV-Cities.

elements.sent-2411-115: Additionally, the presence of water scenes is primarily influenced by the geographical location and climate type of the city.
elements.sent-2411-116: Occlusion scenes, such as those found in tunnels, subway entrances, and overpasses, are relatively scarce.

elements.sent-2411-124: In general, the data of CV-Cities is global in terms of spatial distribution, which provides an excellent foundation for the model to learn the characteristics of different geographical regions around the world.
elements.sent-2411-125: Regarding temporal distribution, the ground and satellite images were predominantly collected within the last five years.

elements.sent-2411-128: The framework employs the DINOv2\cite{ref6 model as the backbone for feature map extractions, addressing the challenges posed by the significant differences in viewpoints.}
elements.sent-2411-129: The mix module\cite{ref7 , which is both efficient and lightweight, is then utilized to aggregate the feature maps, thereby obtaining robust global features of the images.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Two sampling strategies are employed during the training process.
elements.sent-2411-131: Geographically neighboring images are selected as the initial training negative sample images using GPS coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-132: Subsequently, the visual similarity between the image features extracted by the model is leveraged to mine negative samples dynamically during the training process.
elements.sent-2411-133: The symmetric InfoNCE loss\cite{ref8, ref9, ref10 is employed in the framework to facilitate more effective model training.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Furthermore, our network weights are shared, eliminating the need to train two model weights for ground and satellite images.
elements.sent-2411-135: This results in a more lightweight network and ensures that the input sizes of images are identical, obviating the need for complex steps such as polar conversion or image generation.
elements.sent-2411-136: The CVGL framework is illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig4 .}
elements.sent-2411-137: In CVGL tasks, the variability in image perspectives and complex scenes impose significant demands on feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-138: To address these challenges, we use the vision foundational model DINOv2\cite{ref6 as the backbone.}
elements.sent-2411-139: DINOv2 has undergone extensive training on a diverse dataset, enabling it to capture image details and features more effectively than conventional models.
elements.sent-2411-140: Its superior generalization ability ensures robust performance across various tasks, and it offers a broader range of applicable domains compared to previously released models, such as Segment Anything\cite{ref42 .}
elements.sent-2411-141: The architecture of DINOv2 is depicted in Fig. \ref{fig5 .}
elements.sent-2411-142: First, an image of size $W \times H \times C$ is input to the patch embed, which contains a $2D$ convolutional layer with a convolutional kernel of $14 \times 14$ , as well as a normal layer.
elements.sent-2411-143: This module uniformly outputs $(W // 14) \times (H // 14)$ size patches.
elements.sent-2411-144: The patches are then input to the ViT Blocks, and the number of ViT blocks varies according to the model size.
elements.sent-2411-145: Finally, the ViT Blocks output a feature matrix of size $N$ $\times$ $D$.
elements.sent-2411-146: In this study, we remove the layer normalization and head layers and add a feature transformation step to transform the $N \times D$ feature vector matrix into $S$ feature maps of size $h \times w$ as inputs to the mix module.
elements.sent-2411-147: The transformation relation is depicted in (\ref{equation1 ). \begin{equation}\label{equation1} \begin{cases} N = hw D = s \end{cases}\end{equation} Where $N$ and $D$ are the channel count and length of the feature vector respectively, $s$, $h$, and w are the number, height, and width of the respective feature maps.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/figure5.png} \caption{The structures of DINOv2 and feature transformation.} \label{fig5}
elements.sent-2411-148: The training mode of DINOv2 is self-supervision training, which can learn from any image collection and can also learn certain features that cannot be learned by the existing methods, including a total of four sizes of ViTg14 (giant), ViTl14 (large), ViTb14 (base), and ViTs14 (small) models, among which ViTl14, ViTb14 and ViTs14 are ViTg14 obtained through knowledge distillation, inheriting the superior ability of ViTg14, with specific parameters shown in Table \ref{tab2 .}
elements.sent-2411-149: After preliminary experiments, it was found that the CVGL accuracy of ViTg14 and ViTl14 as backbone was not high, and considering the limited computational resources, we primarily selected ViTb14 and ViTs14 as the backbone.

elements.sent-2411-150: Existing state-of-the-art methods primarily employ shallow aggregation layers within the feature-rich layers of cropped pre-trained backbones.
elements.sent-2411-151: However, recent advances in isotropic architectures suggest that self-attention is not essential for vision transformers.
elements.sent-2411-152: The mix module incorporates a global relationship between elements in each feature map in a cascade of feature mixing, eliminating the need for local or pyramidal aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-153: The novel method termed the ``feature mix'', offers a holistic aggregation approach that merges global relationships into each feature map through an iterative process.
elements.sent-2411-154: This technique utilizes a stack of isotropic blocks consisting of multilayer perceptrons (MLPs), collectively known as the feature mixer\cite{ref7 .}
elements.sent-2411-155: This method is demonstrated to achieve high performance with minimal computational burden\cite{ref43 , as supported by various qualitative and quantitative results, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig6 .}}
elements.sent-2411-156: For an input feature map $F\in R^{(s\times h\times w)}$, the mix treats the $3D$ tensor $F\in R^{(s\times h\times w)}$ as a set of $s$ $2D$ features of size h×w as shown in (\ref{equation2 ): \begin{equation}\label{equation2} F=\{X^{i} \}, i=\{1, \ldots,s\}\end{equation} where $X^i$ is the ith feature map.}
elements.sent-2411-157: Next, each $2D$ feature map $X^i$ is then flattened into a $1D$ vector, ultimately leading to an expanded feature map $F\in R^{(s\times n)}$, where $n=h\times w$.
elements.sent-2411-158: These $s$ spread feature maps are fed into the feature mixer, a composition of $L$ MLPs with identical structures (see Fig. \ref{fig6 ).}
elements.sent-2411-159: The feature mixer incorporates spatial global relations into each ${X^i}\in F$ iteratively, as shown in (\ref{equation3 ): \begin{equation}\label{equation3} {X^i}\gets{W_2}{\big(}\sigma({W_1}{X^i} ){\big)}+{X^i},i=\{1, \ldots,s\}\end{equation} where $W_1$ and $W_2$ are the two fully connected layers’ weights that constitute the MLP, and $\sigma$ is the ReLU activation function.}
elements.sent-2411-160: Given an input matrix $F\in R^{(s\times n)}$, the feature mixer’s isotropic design generates an output matrix $Z\in R^{(s\times n)}$.
elements.sent-2411-161: This output passes through subsequent feature mixer blocks until $L$ consecutive blocks have been processed, as shown in (\ref{equation4 ). \begin{equation}\label{equation4} Z={FM_L}{\Big(}FM_{(L-1)}{\big(} \ldots{FM_1} (F){\big)}{\Big)}\end{equation} Both $Z$ and $F$ share the same dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-162: Two fully connected layers sequentially transform the channel dimensions and rows to manage the descriptor's dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-163: Initially, a depth projection maps $Z$ from $R^{(s\times n)}$ to $R^{(d\times n)}$ as shown in (\ref{equation5 ): \begin{equation}\label{equation5} {Z^{'}}={W_d}{\big(}Transpose(Z){\big)}\end{equation} where $W_d$ is the fully connected layer weight.}
elements.sent-2411-164: The output $Z^{'}$ from $R^{(d\times n)}$ is then mapped to $R^{(d\times r)}$ using a line-by-line projection, as shown in (\ref{equation6 ): \begin{equation}\label{equation6} O={W_{r}}{\big(}Transpose({Z^{'}}){\big)}\end{equation} where $W_r$ is the other fully connected layer weight.}
elements.sent-2411-165: The output $O$ with a dimension of $d\times r$ is subsequently expanded and $L2$ normalized to create a global feature vector.
elements.sent-2411-166: Since the model is not initially adapted to the CVGL, thus precludes the selection of negative samples that are proximal to the query images by the model.
elements.sent-2411-167: However, images with close geographic distances exhibit similarities, including architectural styles, vegetation, transportation facilities, road pavement, etc. Consequently, the near neighbor sampling strategy based on GPS location is employed during the initial training phase, enabling the selection of appropriate negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-168: The CV-Cities, CVUSA, and VIGOR images have coordinates under the WGS84 coordinate system.
elements.sent-2411-169: Consequently, the negative samples are chosen based on the haversine distance, whereas the image coordinates of CVACT are under the UTM coordinate system.
elements.sent-2411-170: In this case, the Euclidean distance is utilized to choose negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-171: Following the preliminary training phase, the model has been adapted to the CVGL task.
elements.sent-2411-172: In this context, we employ Dynamic Similarity Sampling (DSS) to select negative samples for training.
elements.sent-2411-173: The initially trained model is first used to calculate the cosine distance between all samples.
elements.sent-2411-174: Subsequently, the top $S$ nearest-neighbor samples with the highest similarity are selected.
elements.sent-2411-175: These $S$ nearest-neighbor samples are then sorted by similarity, and the top $s/2$ are chosen as part of the batch.
elements.sent-2411-176: To enhance the diversity of negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-177: The parameters are set to $s = 64$ and $S = 128$.
elements.sent-2411-178: An alternative to comparison learning is to utilize all available negative samples in the batch instead of the triad loss, known as InfoNCE\cite{ref44 or NTXent loss\cite{ref9 , as shown in (\ref{equation7 ): \begin{equation}\label{equation7} \mathcal{L}(q, R)_{InfoNCE}=-\log \frac{exp(q\cdot r_{+}/\tau )}{\sum _{i=0}^{R}exp(q\cdot r_{i}/\tau )}\end{equation} In this context, $q$ is a coded ground query image, and $R$ is a set of coded satellite reference images.}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: There is only one positive $r_i$, which is $r_+$, matching with $q$.
elements.sent-2411-180: This function computes low values for similar query-positive pairs and high values for dissimilar query-negative pairs.
elements.sent-2411-181: The loss is derived from cross-entropy and is used to gauge the similarity between views.
elements.sent-2411-182: The temperature parameter $\tau$ that can be either optimized or set to a constant value\cite{ref44 .}
elements.sent-2411-183: Historically, the InfoNCE has been applied asymmetrically for unsupervised image representation learning\cite{ref9 .}
elements.sent-2411-184: However, symmetric formulations have proven effective in multimodal pretraining by integrating diverse modalities\cite{ref44 .}
elements.sent-2411-185: Consequently, this loss function is now applied symmetrically to facilitate bidirectional information flow between satellite and ground images.

elements.sent-2411-188: This section presents the specifics of the training and evaluation processes.
elements.sent-2411-189: After that, a comparison is made between our and state-of-the-art methodology.
elements.sent-2411-190: Subsequently, ablation studies are conducted to assess the impact of the backbone structure, the number of mix layers, and the model’s generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-191: Subsequently, the CVGL results for several images are presented, and the precision distribution of our framework in each city is illustrated and analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-192: Finally, heat maps of sample images of typical scenes are generated to analyze the framework’s performance qualitatively.
elements.sent-2411-193: Unless otherwise stated, we use ten city images from CV-Cities for training by default and another six cities to test the model’s CVGL accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-194: Our framework is compared with state-of-the-art on five benchmarks: CV-Cities, CVUSA, CVACT, Universities-1652, and VIGOR.
elements.sent-2411-195: University-1652 contains two modes, Drone2Sat and Sat2Drone, and VIGOR comprises two modes, SAME and CROSS.
elements.sent-2411-196: To compare the accuracy of our method with previous approaches, we employed three evaluation metrics: top-k, average precision (AP), and hit rate, across various datasets.
elements.sent-2411-197: Top-k \cite{ref33, ref45, ref46, ref47 is the metric used to calculate the percentage of predicted locations that the top-k locations with the highest probability contain correct locations.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Specifically, when a list of candidate locations is generated for a query image using the geo-localization method, the image is considered correctly localized if one of the top-k candidates matches the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-199: Top-k is more suitable for datasets with one-to-one ground-truth matching, as expressed in (\ref{equation8 ): \begin{equation}\label{equation8} TopK = \sum _{i=1}^{n}S_{i, k}/n\end{equation} Where $n$ is the number of query images, $i$ is the query image labeled as $i$.}
elements.sent-2411-200: If there is a correct image in the first $k$ results, $S_{(i,k)}$ is $1$, otherwise it is $0$.
elements.sent-2411-201: The commonly used ones are top1, top5, top10, and top1\%.
elements.sent-2411-202: However, in the University-1652, one satellite image corresponds to multiple ground images.
elements.sent-2411-203: Top-k does not adequately reflect the matching results of the rest ground-truth images.
elements.sent-2411-204: Therefore, AP is a better choice.
elements.sent-2411-205: AP\cite{ref18, ref35 is an integral of the precision of each recall threshold, that is, the sum of the areas under the PR curve, as shown in (\ref{equation9 ): \begin{equation}\label{equation9} AP = \sum _{i=1}^{n} (R_i-R_{i-1}) P_i\end{equation} Where $n$ is the number of query images, $R_i$ is the first recall threshold, $P_i$ is the precision rate.}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Hit rate\cite{ref39 , if the retrieved top1 reference image overrides the query image (including ground truth), it is considered a “hit”, the percentage of hits to the total number of queries is the hit rate.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Given the many parameters and training efficiency, we have elected to utilize the ViTb14 and ViTs14 of DINOv2 as the backbone.
elements.sent-2411-208: To this end, we have removed the final layer of normalization and the head layer, incorporated a feature transformation layer to generate the feature maps, and evaluated the framework’s performance with ViTb14 and ViTs14 as a backbone.
elements.sent-2411-209: To meet the experimental design requirements, we gradually release the parameter updates from the mix module to ViT blocks and then to the patch embedding module, aiming to identify the optimal model parameter updating strategy.
elements.sent-2411-210: To complete our experiments, we utilized six RTX4090 GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-211: During model training, we incorporated a label smoothing setting of $0.1$ within the InfoNCE loss to prevent the model from overfitting to the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-212: Since the north direction of the ground query image is not defined during the geo-localization process, a rotation of the north direction of the ground panorama image is performed using horizontal cropping and splicing with a probability of $75\%$ during the training process.
elements.sent-2411-213: This is coupled with a simultaneous rotation of the satellite image to align more closely with the intended application.
elements.sent-2411-214: Random image compression, color jitter, blurring, and sharpening are applied to the ground and satellite images.
elements.sent-2411-215: Random grid-based pixel masking also simulates image occlusion, enhancing the model’s generalizability.
elements.sent-2411-216: The model was trained using the SGD optimizer with a batch size of $32$.
elements.sent-2411-217: The initial learning rate was set to $0.001$, employing a cosine learning rate scheduler with a 1-epoch warm-up period.
elements.sent-2411-218: The training process spanned $40$ epochs uniformly.
elements.sent-2411-219: Our evaluation metrics are consistent with existing papers\cite{ref33, ref45, ref46, ref47 Top-k is the evaluation metric for all datasets in this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-220: For University-1652, we use the AP\cite{ref18, ref35 to evaluate the model precision.}
elements.sent-2411-221: We also use Hit Rate\cite{ref39 as an evaluation metric for VIGOR.}

elements.sent-2411-235: The ViTb14-mix outperforms the state-of-the-art in all evaluation metrics, including top1 and top5 accuracies and hit rates in both SAME and CROSS modes.
elements.sent-2411-236: The significant improvement in CROSS mode, where the training and testing cities are separated, demonstrates our model's capability to better learn the generalizable relationships between ground and satellite images.
elements.sent-2411-237: This improvement can be attributed to our framework's ability to capture complex scene-level information and align features more effectively across disparate views, leading to a $3.84\%$ improvement over Sample4Geo in hit rate for the CROSS setting.
elements.sent-2411-238: The ViTs14-mix also surpasses existing methods with fewer parameters, further reinforcing our model's competitiveness in CVGL tasks.
elements.sent-2411-239: The enhanced performance across all datasets stems from the synergy between our architectural design, sampling strategy, and loss function.
elements.sent-2411-240: The ViTb14-mix and ViTs14-mix leverage the pretrained foundational vision backbone and the mix module to effectively aggregate features across views.
elements.sent-2411-241: The combined NNS and DSS strategies further boost training robustness by selecting high-quality negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-242: Additionally, symmetric InfoNCE loss improves feature alignment, especially in datasets with large intra-class variations like CV-Cities and VIGOR, highlighting the robustness of our framework in diverse cross-view scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-243: The underlying backbone significantly influences the accuracy of CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-244: We trained and tested the model with ViTb14 and ViTs14 as the backbone, with the number of feature mixer layers set to two, respectively, on the CV-Cities, and the results are shown in Table \ref{tab6 .}
elements.sent-2411-245: The test results demonstrate that the model with ViTb14 as the backbone exhibits superior accuracy compared to ViTs14 across all six test cities.

elements.sent-2411-252: As illustrated in Table \ref{tab7 , The accuracy of ViTb14-mix on the CV-Cities demonstrates a gradual improvement as the number of updated parameters in training increases.}
elements.sent-2411-253: The optimal parameter update combination is All, which entails updating all parameters.
elements.sent-2411-254: The accuracy on the CVUSA reaches a plateau, and except for the combination that updates only the parameters of the mix module, the accuracy of the other parameter update combinations is not significantly different.
elements.sent-2411-255: The highest and sub-optimal accuracy parameter update combination about the University-1652 D2S is the Last 4 blocks + mix and the Last 6 blocks + mix.
elements.sent-2411-256: It can be observed that further liberalized parameter updates will increase the accuracy of ViTb14-mix on the CV-Cities.
elements.sent-2411-257: The highest accuracy on the University-1652 D2S is achieved with the Last 4 blocks + mix configuration, after which the accuracy declines with the addition of the liberalization parameter.
elements.sent-2411-258: In conclusion, the optimal parameter updating combinations for ViTb14-mix differ across datasets.
elements.sent-2411-259: Increasing the updated parameters is a suitable approach when the training set (e.g., CV-Cities) is enormous.
elements.sent-2411-260: Conversely, when the training set (e.g., University-1652) is small, freezing some of the pretraining parameters and updating the remaining ones is recommended to achieve the optimal training effect.
elements.sent-2411-261: To prove the effectiveness of the mix and determine the optimal number of mix layers, we used ViTb14 as the backbone and conducted training and testing on CV-Cities with $L$ set to $1$, $2$, $3$, and $4$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-262: Additionally, we replaced the mix with two layers of MLP for comparison with the mix.
elements.sent-2411-263: As shown in Fig. \ref{fig7 , while ViTb14+mlp achieved a higher average top1 accuracy, it exhibited poorer robustness in cities with complex scenes such as Taipei and Singapore.}
elements.sent-2411-264: In contrast, ViTb14+mix demonstrated high robustness across all scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-265: Experimental data indicate that with $L=1$ or $L=2$, the model achieves suboptimal and optimal values for Top1 accuracies across six tested cities.
elements.sent-2411-266: However, increasing the number of mix layers beyond this point results in a decline in test accuracy, suggesting that too many mix layers can impair the model's ability to accurately extract image features.
elements.sent-2411-267: Our findings ultimately suggest that the optimal number of mix layers is $L=2$.

elements.sent-2411-268: To evaluate the effect of the size of the training dataset on the model's generalization ability, we randomly selected a different number $(1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10)$ of cities from the ten training cities of the CV-Cities to train our ViTb14-mix model and tested it on the six test cities of the CV-Cities, and the test results are shown in Fig. \ref{fig8 .}
elements.sent-2411-269: As illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig8 , the model trained using data from a single city exhibits the lowest test accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-270: The model’s accuracy generally exhibits a rapid upward trajectory, followed by a tendency to stabilize, as the number of training cities increases.
elements.sent-2411-271: In general, adding further training cities has a marked positive effect on the model’s accuracy when tested.
elements.sent-2411-272: However, this improvement tends to stabilize after a certain number of training cities have been included.
elements.sent-2411-273: These findings illustrate that CV-Cities possesses substantial global advantages and can provide adequate training for global image CVGL models.
elements.sent-2411-274: Furthermore, the results demonstrate that a moderate amount of training set city data can be selected for training in practical applications, which can ensure the model’s accuracy while reducing the data collection and processing cost.
elements.sent-2411-275: To evaluate the impact of symmetric InfoNCE loss on CVGL performance, we conducted comparative experiments against the widely used soft-margin triplet loss and triplet loss.
elements.sent-2411-276: As presented in Table \ref{tab8 , symmetric InfoNCE loss consistently outperforms the alternatives, with average top1 accuracy improvements of $15.89\%$ and $17.08\%$ over the soft-margin triplet loss and triplet loss, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-277: Moreover, compared to both triplet and soft-margin triplet loss, employing symmetric InfoNCE loss during training results in faster model convergence.
elements.sent-2411-278: Thus, in terms of both efficiency and accuracy, symmetric InfoNCE loss proves to be the optimal choice.

elements.sent-2411-279: To quantitatively evaluate the influence of various sampling strategies on CVGL accuracy, we evaluated four approaches (Random, NNS, DSS, and NNS+DSS) using the CV-Cities.
elements.sent-2411-280: As presented in Table \ref{tab9 , the average top1 accuracy achieved by the DSS and NNS strategies is $74.73\%$ and $68.92\%$, respectively, both representing substantial improvements over the random sampling strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-281: Furthermore, the NNS+DSS strategy achieves a superior average top1 accuracy of $81.91\%$, outperforming all other individual sampling strategies.
elements.sent-2411-282: This result indicates that using the NNS strategy for initial negative sample selection effectively accelerates the model’s adaptation to the CVGL task.
elements.sent-2411-283: Subsequently, incorporating the DSS strategy to select negative samples with high similarity to the query image enables the training of a more robust and accurate model.

elements.sent-2411-286: Tall buildings in city scenes present a significant challenge for CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-287: These structures obstruct the view of ground images and exhibit distinct building texture feature differences in ground and satellite images due to the variation in viewpoint.
elements.sent-2411-288: Our model, trained by CV-Cities, is capable of discerning the relationship between the two viewpoint images typical of an urban scene and subsequently identifying the geographic position of the query image.. Season change.
elements.sent-2411-289: As illustrated in the exemplar image, the query and reference images were captured in winter and summer, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-290: Similar vegetation in both images presents a significant challenge for CVGL, as the vegetation exhibits notable differences between the two seasons.
elements.sent-2411-291: Despite the challenges mentioned above, our model can still get the real location.. Occlusion.
elements.sent-2411-292: With occlusions at the top, our model can accurately match the corresponding satellite images based on the implied rules and features and thus locate the query image's geographic location accurately.. Nature scene.
elements.sent-2411-293: The presence of fewer artificial texture features is offset by the prevalence of natural features such as vegetation and topography, which are less regular and more repetitive, so CVGL is more difficult.
elements.sent-2411-294: This example demonstrates that our model can accurately locate the query image’s geographic position through the relationship between features and space.. Water scene.
elements.sent-2411-295: The lack of distinctive features on the water surface and the prevalence of repetitive textures render the water area particularly challenging for CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-296: The limited scope for distinguishing between areas, such as the coastline and riverbanks, further exacerbates this difficulty.
elements.sent-2411-297: In this case, our model demonstrates a notable deficiency.
elements.sent-2411-298: However, the top$5$ exhibits a noteworthy capability: it can retrieve satellite images of nearby locations within $200 m$ with a high probability.. Cloud sat view.
elements.sent-2411-299: Cloud sat view is a case of CVGL for Taipei, China.
elements.sent-2411-300: However, it suffers from poor localization accuracy due to several factors, including clouds and fog in satellite images.
elements.sent-2411-301: This is an area that requires further investigation.
elements.sent-2411-302: One potential avenue for improvement is to enhance the images through the removal of fog and the reduction of noise to improve the geo-localization accuracy.

elements.sent-2411-322: This study integrates a vision foundational model with feature mixing to achieve high-precision CVGL and introduces a novel global dataset, CV-Cities, which significantly enhances the model's capability for global CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-323: Furthermore, the diverse and complex scenes within CV-Cities establish new benchmarks for CVGL evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-324: Experiments in Section \ref{subection_Comparison_to_the_State-of-the-ar illustrate that our framework demonstrates greater robustness in challenging environments, such as city scenes, natural scenes, occlusions, and seasonal changes, outperforming existing CVGL methods\cite{ref2, ref8, ref28, ref38, ref39, ref40 .}}
elements.sent-2411-325: Compared to Sample4Geo\cite{ref8 (SOTA), our framework achieves an average improvement of $3.09\%$ in top1 accuracy across the CV-Cities dataset and several existing datasets\cite{ref19, ref34, ref35, ref36 .}}
elements.sent-2411-326: Notably, on the CV-Cities, which presents more complex scenarios, the accuracy improvement reaches $8.42\%$.
elements.sent-2411-327: Unlike CVUSA and CVACT, the CV-Cities is larger, more geographically diverse, and features a wider range of scenes, greatly enhancing the model's generalization capabilities (see Section \ref{subsubsection_Generalization_Capabilities ).}
elements.sent-2411-328: Additionally, as demonstrated in Section \ref{subsubsection_Loss_Comparison , the introduction of the symmetric InfoNCE loss boosts accuracy by $17.08\%$ and $15.89\%$ compared to triplet loss and soft-margin triplet loss, respectively, while also improving training efficiency.}

elements.sent-2411-331: However, a notable limitation of our framework is its reliance on ground panoramic images, which restricts the model's adaptability to perspective views.
elements.sent-2411-332: Moreover, as detailed in Sections \ref{subsubsection_CVGL_Samples and \ref{subsubsection_Precision_Distribution , repetitive textures in water bodies and cloud obstructions in satellite images pose significant challenges to the performance of our framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-333: Future work will focus on overcoming these limitations by enhancing and preprocessing the CV-Cities, particularly by converting ground panoramic images into perspective formats for model training.
elements.sent-2411-334: This approach will improve the model's ability to handle perspective imagery and achieve more accurate CVGL.
elements.sent-2411-335: Additionally, image enhancement techniques, including defogging and noise reduction, will be implemented to alleviate the adverse effects caused by clouds and fog in satellite imagery, thereby improving localization accuracy under these challenging conditions.
elements.sent-2411-336: The CV-Cities dataset also has limitations, particularly in its geographic coverage.
elements.sent-2411-337: Due to data availability constraints, certain regions, including North Africa, the Middle East, and Central Asia, lack sufficient street view imagery.
elements.sent-2411-338: This limitation hinders the construction of datasets for cities in these regions, which slightly reduces the overall representativeness of the CV-Cities.
elements.sent-2411-339: Moving forward, we plan to expand the CV-Cities by collecting data from representative cities in China mainland, further enhancing its diversity and overall representativeness.
elements.sent-2411-340: This work proposes a novel CVGL framework that combines the vision foundational model DINOv2 and the feature mix module.
elements.sent-2411-341: The test accuracy of our framework is enhanced by incorporating the symmetric InfoNCE loss and the sampling strategies of near neighbor sampling and dynamic similarity sampling.
elements.sent-2411-342: Furthermore, to address the limitations of existing datasets, we have built a global CVGL dataset, CV-Cities.
elements.sent-2411-343: The dataset encompasses sixteen cities across six continents and includes many complex scenarios, rendering it a challenging global CVGL benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-344: The experimental results demonstrate that the model trained on the CV-Cities exhibits high accuracy in diverse test cities, fulfilling the global CVGL requirements.
